title: Build the Foundation
description: These aren't transition tasks — they're career tasks. The earlier you start, the stronger your position when it's time to hang up the uniform.

items.task: Define your purpose and post-military goals
items.details: What do you want your life to look like after the uniform? Write it down. Share it with someone who matters. This drives everything else.

items.task: Open and fund your TSP account — contribute at least 5% (BRS)
items.details: Start contributing on day one. BRS members: 5% minimum to get full government match. Compound interest is your biggest weapon — every year you delay costs you tens of thousands.

items.task: Start your degree using Tuition Assistance
items.details: Use TA ($250/credit hour, $4,500/year) for your bachelor's. Don't touch the GI Bill. Find schools that match TA rates. TA has service-specific eligibility cutoffs — use it while you can.

items.task: Kill your debt
items.details: Every dollar in debt payments is a dollar that isn't building your future. Attack high-interest debt first. Stay away from predatory lenders near base.

items.task: Transfer GI Bill to dependents
items.details: Requires 6+ years of service AND a 4-year commitment from the transfer date. Transfer at least 1 month to each dependent to get them in the system. You CANNOT add new dependents after separation.

items.task: Document medical conditions — go to sick call for EVERYTHING
items.details: Every time something hurts, get it on record. This is the foundation for your VA disability claim. The best evidence is contemporaneous medical records from active duty.

items.task: Pursue in-service postgraduate programs
items.details: NPS, AFIT, or civilian grad school on the military's dime. They pay tuition AND your salary AND it counts toward retirement. If available, take it.

items.task: Build relationships and find mentors
items.details: Network with people in fields you're interested in. Build relationships up, down, and sideways. Your post-military career starts being built now.

items.task: Pursue relevant certifications
items.details: If your role gives you access to cert courses (GPEN, CISSP, PMP, etc.), take them while they're free or funded. That window might be shorter than you think.

items.task: Collect letters of recommendation throughout your career
items.details: Get them while people remember you. Every CO, department head, chief, or peer who can vouch for your character and competence. Don't wait until the last month — people PCS, retire, and forget.

items.task: Sign up for USMAP (if your job qualifies)
items.details: Converts your on-the-job military training into nationally recognized Department of Labor apprenticeship certificates. Free credential — sign up early, it tracks hours over time.

title: Get Serious About Transition
description: Transition is no longer theoretical. Start making concrete moves.

items.task: Attend TAP — Individualized Initial Counseling
items.details: One-on-one session with a TAP counselor. Complete self-assessment, begin your Individual Transition Plan (ITP), initiate DD Form 2648, determine your tier level.

items.task: Research SkillBridge programs
items.details: SkillBridge lets you work for a civilian employer during your last 180 days while keeping military pay. Start researching companies and programs now — command approval takes time.

items.task: Calculate your Financial Freedom Number
items.details: Annual expenses x 25 = your number. Factor in pension, VA disability, TSP, and investments. Know exactly where you stand.

items.task: Research state veteran benefits where you plan to live
items.details: Property tax exemptions, education benefits for dependents, employment preferences — these vary wildly by state and can influence where you choose to settle.

items.task: Start building your resume
items.details: Translate your military experience into civilian language. No jargon. Focus on outcomes and impact, not duties. Get it reviewed by civilians in your target industry.

title: Execute Your Plan
description: One year to go. Time to execute, not just plan.

items.task: Complete TAP Core Curriculum
items.details: Three mandatory days: DoD/DHS Transition Day (Managing Your Transition, MOC Crosswalk, Financial Planning), VA Day, and DOL Employment Workshop. Actually engage — don't blow these off.

items.task: Update your LinkedIn profile
items.details: Professional headshot (no uniform), civilian-friendly headline, translated experience. Start connecting with people in your target field.

items.task: Request your SMART transcript / military training records
items.details: Your military training converts to college credits. Get your transcript and see what transfers. Army: AARTS. Navy/Marines: SMART. Air Force: CCAF.

items.task: Review your personnel records for accuracy
items.details: Check your service record, awards, qualifications. Fix errors now — it's much harder after you're out.

items.task: Apply for SkillBridge (if using)
items.details: Submit your SkillBridge application with enough lead time for command approval. Some commands need 6+ months notice.

title: BDD Window Opens — FILE NOW
description: This is critical. Your VA disability claims window opens NOW. Don't wait.

items.task: FILE YOUR VA DISABILITY CLAIM (BDD)
items.details: Benefits Delivery at Discharge (BDD) window: 180-90 days before separation. File NOW. Use a VSO (free). This is not optional — your rating can mean thousands per month tax-free for the rest of your life.

items.task: Connect with a Veterans Service Organization (VSO)
items.details: DAV, VFW, American Legion — they provide free claims representatives who know the system. Let them help you file.

items.task: Schedule and complete all C&P exams
items.details: Compensation & Pension exams are how the VA rates your conditions. Don't miss them. Don't minimize symptoms. Be honest about how conditions affect your daily life.

items.task: Begin SkillBridge (if approved)
items.details: 180 days of working for a civilian employer at full military pay. This is your tryout — and theirs. Treat it like the job interview it is.

items.task: Start applying for civilian jobs
items.details: Many employers want someone who can start in 2-4 weeks. Time your applications accordingly. Too early and they'll pass. Too late and you'll have a gap.

items.task: Research healthcare transition options
items.details: TAMP (180 days free TRICARE after separation), TRICARE Retiree (if 20-year), VA healthcare enrollment, CHCBP, employer insurance. Know what covers you and your family on day one.

title: BDD Window Closing — Final Push
description: Last chance to file disability claims under BDD. Lock in your plan.

items.task: LAST CHANCE: File any remaining VA disability claims
items.details: BDD window closes at 90 days before separation. If you haven't filed, do it TODAY. After this window, the process takes much longer and you lose the expedited timeline.

items.task: Complete TAP Capstone
items.details: Review of your Career Readiness Standards (CRS). Confirm all requirements are met. Get warm handovers for anything outstanding.

items.task: Plan your move
items.details: Schedule movers, arrange housing, update DEERS address. If retiring, plan your final move carefully — you only get one.

items.task: Set up your financial safety net
items.details: Have 3-6 months of expenses in savings. Know exactly when your first pension check, VA disability payment, and civilian paycheck will arrive. Plan for the gap between last military paycheck and first civilian one.

items.task: Apply for state veteran education benefits
items.details: Conroy (MD), Hazlewood (TX), and dozens of other state programs. Research what's available in the state you're moving to. Some have annual application deadlines — don't miss them.

title: Wrapping Up
description: Almost there. Tie up loose ends and don't rush the important stuff.

items.task: Review your DD-214 CAREFULLY before signing
items.details: This is the most important document of your military career. Check every entry: dates, awards, characterization of service, RE code. Errors are extremely difficult to fix later.

items.task: Enroll in VA healthcare
items.details: Even if you have other insurance, enroll. You can do this online at va.gov/health-care/apply/. Don't wait.

items.task: Verify TRICARE transition coverage
items.details: TAMP: 180 days of premium-free TRICARE after qualifying separations. Retirees: TRICARE Prime/Select continues (enrollment fees apply). Know your coverage start date.

items.task: Decide what to do with TSP
items.details: You can leave money in TSP (lowest fees in the industry), roll to an IRA, or roll to a new employer's 401(k). Don't cash it out — the tax penalty and lost growth aren't worth it.

items.task: Update beneficiaries on all accounts
items.details: SGLI converts to VGLI (if you want it). Update beneficiaries on TSP, life insurance, bank accounts. Make sure everything reflects your post-military situation.

title: You're Out — Now Execute
description: The transition doesn't end on your last day. Stay on top of these.

items.task: Verify VA disability rating and payment schedule
items.details: Check va.gov for your rating decision. Payments typically start the month after separation. Disability pay arrives on the 1st. If BDD claim isn't decided yet, be patient — it's processing.

items.task: File for Chapter 35 DEA (once you receive 100% P&T rating)
items.details: If your rating comes back as 100% Permanent and Total, file Ch. 35 for your dependents. This is a separate 36-month entitlement for each eligible dependent that does NOT consume your GI Bill.

items.task: Obtain your veteran ID card
items.details: Apply at va.gov. Also get a VA health ID card at your local VA medical center.

items.task: Register with your state's Department of Veterans Affairs
items.details: State-level benefits (property tax exemptions, hunting/fishing licenses, education programs) often require state VA registration.

items.task: Apply for NGO/organization scholarships for dependents
items.details: Folds of Honor, American Legion Legacy, Fisher House, ThanksUSA — these stack on top of federal and state benefits. Apply to all you qualify for.

items.task: Stay connected with the veteran community
items.details: The camaraderie doesn't have to end. VSOs, veteran groups, online communities — find your people. The transition can feel isolating if you let it.

items.task: Talk to someone if you need to
items.details: Transition anxiety is real. You don't have to power through alone. Veterans Crisis Line: 988 (press 1). Military OneSource: 800-342-9647.
